text:
I've now read the vocabulary and all four Fieldwork fixtures. The vocabulary file is scoped to the Verdant demo components (`care-task-row`, `demo-notice`, `plant-card`, `primary-button`, `screen-header`, `stat-tile`, `status-chip`) plus one library-generic primitive, `metric-tile` (`ds-` prefix), whose own usage note explicitly calls out "the agentic slots in Fieldwork's dispatch canvas" as an intended use. Since the insight-panel slot wants "a vertical column of metric-tile nodes," and no Fieldwork-specific components exist in this vocabulary, `metric-tile` is the only — and correct — component to compose with here.

Computing from `jobs.json` with completedAt === null as "open," against today 2026-07-14 and the board's own 2-day SLA window (slaDue ≤ 2026-07-16):

- Open backlog total: 35 open jobs.
- Overdue (status "overdue", already breached): 4 (job-002, job-023, job-036, job-046).
- SLA-at-risk (open, slaDue ≤ 2026-07-16 — includes the overdue 4 plus 4 more due imminently: job-006, job-030, job-061, job-062): 8.
- Priority mix among the 35 open jobs: urgent 9, priority 15, routine 11 (sums to 35, confirmed by manual tally).

I plan six `metric-tile` nodes in a flat array (no nesting — metric-tile has no allowed children): Open backlog (neutral), Overdue (critical), SLA at risk (warn), Urgent priority (warn), Priority tier (neutral), Routine tier (neutral). Labels are drawn from copy.json's `statusLabels.overdue`, `slaWarningLabel`, and `priorityLabels` where they fit, otherwise phrased to name the metric plainly. Values will be strings per the schema (e.g. "35"), with unit "jobs" throughout.

text:
Re-reading the vocabulary confirms `metric-tile` is the correct (and only viable) primitive: its prop set is exactly `{label: string required, value: string required, unit: string optional, tone: enum[neutral|warn|critical] optional}`, its `children` array is empty (so no child nodes are permitted — my flat, unnested array is correct), and its enum for `tone` is `["neutral","warn","critical"]`, which is all I use. The composition rule confirms a composition may be a bare array of `{name, props}` nodes, so six top-level `metric-tile` nodes with no `children` key is a valid shape.

Checking each label against the "reads the state without its tone" rule: "Open backlog" (count of what's open — descriptive, tone-free), "Overdue" (names the breached state itself, matching `statusLabels.overdue` in copy.json), "SLA at risk" (verbatim from copy.json's `slaWarningLabel`, names the at-risk state), "Urgent priority" / "Priority tier" / "Routine tier" (each names the priority band being counted, matching copy.json's `priorityLabels`). None of these labels lean on tone to make their point — the tone props (critical on Overdue, warn on SLA-at-risk and Urgent priority) are redundant emphasis only, satisfying the rule. Three of six tiles carry a tone and three are neutral, which matches the slot's "several toned" guidance without over-coloring the panel.

I double-checked my arithmetic against the raw records again during this gate pass: 35 open jobs (completedAt null), of which 4 carry status "overdue" and 8 fall at or before the 2026-07-16 SLA window (the 4 overdue plus job-006, job-030, job-061, job-062). Priority split of the 35 open jobs: urgent 9, priority 15, routine 11, which sums correctly to 35. Values are all short numeric strings, not sentences, and units are all "jobs" — no invented figures, nothing corrected from the plan.

Nothing needs to change from the plan: component, props, and enums all match exactly, and the six-tile column fits comfortably inside the insight-panel's 3–6-tile bound.

I wrote the six-tile composition to `proto/compositions/backlog-urgency.json` immediately after opening this phase, per the rule that Write only happens under the implement marker. The array is flat — six `metric-tile` nodes, each with `label`, `value` (as a display string), `unit: "jobs"`, and `tone` — giving the panel a top-to-bottom urgency read: total backlog, the two breach-related readings (Overdue, SLA at risk), then the three-way priority split (Urgent / Priority / Routine) that answers the "priority mix" half of the question.

Now validating against the real vocabulary loader.

The exact validate command printed `composition valid` on the first run — no refusal, no path-specific error to fix. This confirms every node name (`metric-tile`), every prop key (`label`, `value`, `unit`, `tone`), and every enum value (`neutral`, `warn`, `critical`) matches the vocabulary exactly, and that the flat six-node array respects the composition shape rules (no illegal children on a component whose `children` list is empty).

The finished panel answers the question directly: "Open backlog" (35) frames the scale, "Overdue" (4, critical) and "SLA at risk" (8, warn) name how urgent it is against the board's own 2-day SLA window, and "Urgent priority" (9, warn) / "Priority tier" (15) / "Routine tier" (11) — summing back to 35 — give the priority mix. All six values were tallied directly from `jobs.json`'s `completedAt`, `status`, `slaDue`, and `priority` fields against the fixed fictional today (2026-07-14), nothing invented.
